claimed_contribution: Proposes an integrated product line optimization procedure that concurrently handles key marketing and engineering criteria rather than treating them separately or sequentially.
Introduces explicit modeling of strategic price reactions by incumbent manufacturers and the retailer when a new product line is launched.
Provides a method that can search large design spaces with mixed discrete and continuous variables, and shows in an empirical case that it yields higher profit than sequential benchmark approaches.
context: The paper studies product line design for consumer durables, with an empirical application to a U.S. power tool development project at a major manufacturer. The market setting includes incumbent brands sold through a dominant retailer whose pricing response is modeled.
design_and_data: The study develops an optimization framework with an outer loop that iterates between the focal manufacturer's design-variable search and retailer/manufacturer price adjustments until profit convergence. Marketing inputs are estimated using a choice-based finite-mixture Bayesian conjoint model from 740 U.S. power tool users, each completing 18 choice tasks with two products plus a no-choice option; the selected demand model has two segments. Engineering inputs come from simulation-based mappings from design variables to consumer attributes and engineering performance outputs, with feasibility, robustness, capacity, maximum line-length, and retailer-acceptance constraints, plus a component-sharing cost model. Evidence includes a simulation study comparing GA, simulated annealing, and analytical target cascading across problem sizes, and an empirical comparison against marketing-first and engineering-first sequential benchmarks.
focal_constructs: mixed discrete and continuous design variables
main_findings: In simulation, GA and simulated annealing produced comparable profit levels, but GA required much less CPU time; for continuous-only problems, analytical target cascading performed strongly and improved relative to heuristics as problem size increased.

Across tested simulated problem sizes, the full iterative procedure converged, with total computation time roughly ranging from 2 to 10.4 hours; in small problems where enumeration was feasible, average earnings were about 96% of the global optimum.

In the power tool application, the integrated method selected a three-product line with estimated 5-year discounted profit of $52.8 million, outperforming the marketing-first benchmark ($46.5 million) and the engineering-first benchmark ($48.3 million).
one_line_summary: The paper proposes and validates an integrated optimization method for consumer durable product lines that jointly accounts for demand heterogeneity, engineering constraints, cost synergy, and channel and competitor price reactions.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior product line design research by unifying heterogeneous conjoint demand, engineering feasibility and robustness, platform cost interactions, and strategic retailer and competitor pricing responses within one optimization framework that accommodates mixed discrete and continuous design variables.
research_gap: Existing product line work is largely discipline-centric, often focusing on either marketing or engineering and not optimizing their interdependent requirements concurrently. The paper argues that prior methods also generally omit strategic pricing responses by incumbents and the retailer and are limited in handling large mixed discrete/continuous design spaces.
research_question: How can a firm design a profit-maximizing product line for consumer durables when marketing and engineering decisions are tightly interdependent? The paper asks how to jointly choose product design configurations and pricing while accounting for heterogeneous consumer preferences, engineering feasibility and robustness, component-sharing cost synergies, and strategic price reactions from incumbents and the retailer.
